summary: An Amazon product page for 'Living on Paper: Letters from Iris Murdoch 1934-1995'. The page includes the publisher's description and customer reviews, which surface a handful of direct quotes from Murdoch's letters regarding her views on love, philosophy, and her personal relationships.

principles.statement: Love requires recognizing the independent reality of others.
principles.rationale: It is an extremely difficult process to move beyond one's own ego and acknowledge that something other than oneself truly exists.
principles.source_quote: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real

principles.statement: Friendship, love, and sex are inextricably linked.
principles.rationale: Emotional and physical connections cannot be neatly compartmentalized or divided from one another.
principles.source_quote: I can't divide friendship from love or love from sex--or sex from love, etc.

signature_quotes.text: Love is the extremely difficult realisation that something other than oneself is real
signature_quotes.context: Quoted in the publisher's description of her letters.

signature_quotes.text: I can't divide friendship from love or love from sex--or sex from love, etc.
signature_quotes.context: From a letter written in the mid-1960s, reflecting on her personal relationships.

signature_quotes.text: Of course schoolchildren should never be allowed to come near philosophy.
I think the Beatles ought to be jointly Poet Laureate.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: She believed that 'schoolchildren should never be allowed to come near philosophy.'
She found the introduction of women's studies majors at Oxford to be 'revolting' and 'ghastly'.
She despised his views and explicitly refused to consider him a 'philosopher'.
